sections.title: 더 (More) + Adjective
sections.explanation: The simplest way to express comparison in Korean is to place 더 (more) before the adjective or verb. Unlike English, which changes the adjective form (big → bigger) or adds 'more,' Korean always uses the same word 더 regardless of the adjective. 더 크다 means 'to be bigger' (literally 'to be more big'). 더 비싸다 means 'to be more expensive.' 더 빨라요 means 'it is faster.' This pattern works with every adjective — you never change the adjective itself. You can also use 더 with action verbs: 더 먹다 (to eat more), 더 공부하다 (to study more).
sections.tip: Korean comparatives are easy — just put 더 before any adjective. No special endings or adjective changes needed. 더 is always 더, no matter the adjective.

examples.en: This bag is more expensive. (더 + 비싸다)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'It is faster' using 빠르다 (to be fast)?
exercises.explanation: Place 더 before the conjugated adjective: 더 빨라요. 빠르다 conjugates to 빨라요 (ㅡ drop, bright vowel ㅏ → 아요), then 더 goes in front.
더 always comes before the adjective: 더 맛있는 (more delicious, modifier form). The order is always 더 + adjective.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I want to sleep more' using 자다 (to sleep)?
exercises.explanation: 더 goes before the verb: 더 자고 싶어요 (I want to sleep more). 더 works with action verbs too, not just adjectives.

sections.title: A보다 B가 더... (B Is More... Than A)
sections.explanation: To make a full comparison sentence in Korean, use the particle 보다 (than) attached to the thing being compared against. The structure is: A보다 B가 더 + adjective. This means 'B is more [adjective] than A.' For example: 사과보다 바나나가 더 싸요 (Bananas are cheaper than apples — literally 'Than apples, bananas are more cheap'). Notice the word order is opposite to English: the 'than' part comes first. The 더 is optional but commonly included for clarity. You can also rearrange: B가 A보다 더 + adjective. Both orders are natural.
sections.tip: 보다 attaches directly to the noun (no space): 사과보다, not 사과 보다. Think of 보다 as 'compared to' — it marks the baseline of comparison.

examples.en: The subway is faster than the bus.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Coffee is hotter than tea'? (커피 = coffee, 차 = tea, 뜨겁다 = hot)
exercises.explanation: A보다 B가 더 + adjective. Tea (차) is the baseline (A), coffee (커피) is the one that is more hot (B): 차보다 커피가 더 뜨거워요.

exercises.prompt: What does 형보다 동생이 더 키가 커요 mean?
options: The older brother is taller than the younger sibling.
The younger sibling is taller than the older brother.
The older brother and younger sibling are the same height.
exercises.explanation: 형보다 (than the older brother) 동생이 (the younger sibling) 더 키가 커요 (is taller). The younger sibling is taller than the older brother.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 한국어___ 일본어가 더 어려워요. (Japanese is harder than Korean.)
exercises.explanation: 보다 marks 'than': 한국어보다 일본어가 더 어려워요 means 'Japanese is harder than Korean.' 보다 attaches to the baseline (Korean).

exercises.prompt: Rearrange into a correct comparison: 'Seoul is bigger than Busan' (서울, 부산, 크다)
exercises.explanation: Seoul is bigger, so Seoul is B (the one that is 'more'). Busan is A (the baseline): 부산보다 서울이 더 커요. Option C would also be grammatically correct with the rearranged order, but it says Busan is bigger than Seoul.

sections.title: 가장/제일 (Most/Best) for Superlatives
sections.explanation: Korean superlatives are formed by placing 가장 or 제일 before the adjective. 가장 is slightly more formal/written and 제일 is more colloquial, but both are used in everyday speech. 가장 큰 means 'the biggest,' 제일 맛있는 means 'the most delicious.' Unlike English, there is no special ending like '-est' — you simply add 가장 or 제일 before the adjective. These work in both predicate position (가장 커요 — is the biggest) and modifier position (가장 큰 집 — the biggest house). You can add 중에서 (among) to specify the group: 반에서 제일 키가 커요 (the tallest in the class).
sections.tip: 가장 and 제일 are interchangeable in most cases. Use 가장 in writing or formal speech, 제일 in casual conversation. Both go right before the adjective.

examples.en: What is the most delicious food?
I'm the tallest in our class.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the cheapest' using 싸다 (to be cheap)?
exercises.explanation: Place 가장 before the modifier form of the adjective: 가장 싼 (the cheapest). 싸다 → modifier 싼, then 가장 싼.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence means 'This is the most interesting book'? (재미있다 = interesting, 책 = book)
exercises.explanation: 가장 goes before the adjective, not before the noun: 가장 재미있는 책 (the most interesting book). 가장 modifies the adjective 재미있는.

exercises.prompt: What does 제일 좋아하는 색깔 mean?
options: Favorite color (the color one likes the most)
exercises.explanation: 제일 좋아하는 literally means 'the most liked.' 제일 좋아하는 색깔 = the color (색깔) that you like (좋아하는) the most (제일) = favorite color.

sections.title: A만큼 B (As Much As A)
sections.explanation: 만큼 is used to express equality or similarity in degree — 'as much as' or 'to the extent of.' It attaches to a noun: A만큼 means 'as much as A.' For example: 언니만큼 예뻐요 (as pretty as my older sister). 나만큼 잘해요 (does it as well as me). You can also use it with verbs in modifier form: 생각한 만큼 어렵지 않아요 (it's not as hard as I thought). In negative sentences, A만큼 B가 못하다 means 'B cannot match A': 형만큼 못해요 (I can't do it as well as my older brother). 만큼 can also mean 'to the extent that' or 'as much as' when following a clause.
sections.tip: 만큼 expresses 'equal to' — think of it as setting a benchmark. 언니만큼 = 'to the level of older sister.' In negative form, it means 'not as much as.'

examples.en: She is as pretty as her older sister.
He is as tall as me.
It's not as cold as yesterday.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'as fast as a car' using 차 (car)?
exercises.explanation: 만큼 means 'as much as': 차만큼 빨라요 (as fast as a car). 보다 would mean 'faster than a car,' which is a different meaning.

options: I'm as good as my older brother.
I can't do it as well as my older brother.
My older brother can't do it.
exercises.explanation: 만큼 + 못하다 means 'cannot match the level of': 형만큼 못해요 = I can't do it as well as my older brother.

exercises.prompt: Fill in: 생각한 ___ 쉬웠어요. (It was as easy as I thought.)
exercises.explanation: 만큼 follows the modifier clause 생각한 (that I thought): 생각한 만큼 쉬웠어요 (it was as easy as I thought).

sections.title: A보다 B가 덜... (B Is Less... Than A)
sections.explanation: 덜 means 'less' and works exactly like 더 (more) but in the opposite direction. Place 덜 before the adjective to express 'less [adjective].' 덜 춥다 means 'to be less cold.' Combined with 보다: 어제보다 덜 추워요 (it's less cold than yesterday). 덜 is commonly used with negative or unpleasant adjectives: 덜 아파요 (it hurts less), 덜 피곤해요 (less tired), 덜 어려워요 (less difficult). While grammatically correct with positive adjectives too, Koreans tend to rephrase — instead of 덜 예쁘다 (less pretty), they might say 더 못생기다 (uglier) or use a different construction. 덜 is especially natural in contexts of improvement or relief.
sections.tip: 덜 is the opposite of 더. It sounds most natural with negative/unpleasant qualities: 덜 아프다, 덜 춥다, 덜 바쁘다. For positive qualities, Koreans usually rephrase rather than using 덜.

examples.en: Today is less cold than yesterday.
It hurts less because I took medicine.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Today is less busy than yesterday'? (바쁘다 = busy)
exercises.explanation: 어제보다 (than yesterday) 덜 바빠요 (is less busy): 오늘이 어제보다 덜 바빠요 (Today is less busy than yesterday).

exercises.prompt: What does 더 and 덜 have in common?
options: Both go before the adjective and modify degree
exercises.explanation: Both 더 (more) and 덜 (less) are degree adverbs that go before the adjective. 더 increases the degree, 덜 decreases it.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence sounds most natural in Korean?
options: 그 사람이 덜 예뻐요. (That person is less pretty.)
약을 먹으니까 덜 아파요. (It hurts less after taking medicine.)
이 음식이 덜 맛있어요. (This food is less delicious.)
exercises.explanation: 덜 sounds most natural with unpleasant states like pain, cold, or difficulty. 덜 아프다 (hurts less) is very natural. For positive adjectives like 예쁘다 or 맛있다, Koreans tend to rephrase.
